name: Kur (Underworld)
shortDescription: Kur, in Sumerian mythology, refers to both the underworld and a primordial monster or dragon inhabiting it. It represents the dark, chaotic forces that predate the organized cosmos.
description:
The Kur is a multifaceted concept in Sumerian cosmology. Primarily, it denotes the underworld, the land of the dead, a dark and desolate realm beneath the earth. It is often described as a place of no return, where the spirits of the deceased exist in a shadowy, diminished state. The journey to the Kur was fraught with peril, requiring passage through guarded gates and across a river of souls. 

Beyond its function as the underworld, Kur also embodies a primordial entity, a monstrous dragon or mountain, that challenged the gods' authority and the established cosmic order. This aspect of Kur represents the chaotic forces that existed before the creation of the world as we know it. Myths describe battles between the gods and Kur, symbolizing the triumph of order over chaos. The defeat of Kur by gods like Enlil or Ninurta was crucial for maintaining the stability of the cosmos. The name is also associated with foreign lands and mountains, hinting at a perception of the unknown as dangerous and chaotic. The concept is closely tied to Sumerian views on death, the afterlife, and the struggle to maintain cosmic balance against primordial threats.

sources.title: The Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature

sources.title: Myths of Enki, the Crafty God
